5: <header>ABSTRACT </header>

This study presents the detailed life cycle assessment (LCA) of environmental impact po- tential for two-stage anaerobic biogas plants between commercial and pilot scales. It provides a comprehensive and systematic approach to evaluating the environmental im- pacts of producing biogas from livestock wastes in several phases: collecting, processing, and production. The commercial biogas plant (C-BP) system is located in Central Taiwan's pig farm, with 1500-2000 pigs a year. The Symnergy (symbiosis bioenergy model) biogas

0: Energy usage and CO2 emissions have become a more signif- icant part of the lifecycle dialogue spurred by the growing concern about climate change. Globally, energy supply and shortages are the most critical environmental pollution and greenhouse gas (GHG) issues [1]. Such issues can be solved using abundant and environmentally friendly renewable en- ergy sources [2,3]. A transition to cleaner energy has already begun in many countries. Despite technological innovation and cost reduction's rapid growth, the competition between renewable sources and energy efficiency technologies and highly subsidized carbon-intensive energy technologies is still tight. Indonesia joined the United Nations Climate Change Conference (COP 26) in Glasgow in 2022 last year. The commitment of Global Methane Pledge was signed by 111 countries representing more than 70% of the global economy, almost half of all methane emissions from anthropogenic. The Global Methane Pledge in COP 26 in Glasgow, UK, commits signatories to reduce emissions by 30% by 2030, compared with 2020. While international climate summits usually focus primarily on carbon dioxide, methane is responsible for about 30% of global warming. Data from the Environmental Protec- tion Administration Taiwan in the National Greenhouse Gas Inventory Report showed that Taiwan emitted 287.09 MtCOe in 2019 and that 90% was CO2. Mainly methane was emitted from agriculture and waste. The new initiative emphasizes making cuts by tackling methane leaking from fossil fuel infrastructure and other sources, such as livestock farming and waste in landfill sites.

Biogas technology effectively converts animal manure to biogas and fertilizer through anaerobic digestion [4,5]. Anaerobic digestion (AD) technology is an effective emission mitigation strategy to decrease the negative environmental impacts of livestock manure management. The stored biogas reduces the methane released into the atmosphere and de- creases dependence on fossil fuels [6]. AD is also considered a

promising alternative for treating slaughterhouse and organic wastes for bioenergy production, such as biomethane or biogas. It also has valuable byproducts, such as biofertilizers or inorganic nutrients, that can be utilized to reduce GHG emissions [7]. Animal waste anaerobic digestion converts organic matter into biogas. The renewable energy source, digested slurry known as digestate as a byproduct, can be used as a biofertilizer in agriculture plantations [8,9]. Various waste products include livestock animal waste, slaughterhouse waste, agricultural waste, animal manure, wastewater, and other sectors. Biogas energy generation has been utilized to solve waste management and energy lack ness problems [9]. Wang et al. [10] found that the treatment of slaughterhouse waste using AD has energy production and GHG emission reduction of about 22 106 GJ and kg- CO2-eq per year, respectively.

There are three types of biogas production systems in terms of the size of the biogas digester: large-scale, medium- scale, and small-scale. Generally, large-scale and medium- scale biogas digester is utilized commercially, and the small- scale has been built on a pilot scale. Usually, a commercial- scale biogas plant system is built using an automation sys- tem with mechanization and electrification as a sophisticated biogas production system. It is often used in intensive farming or ranching, with a digester capacity of 30 m³ or more. On the other hand, the biogas pilot plant is used in small areas such as slaughterhouses or ranch, with the production chain slaughterhouse waste-pretreatment-fermentation capacity starting at 2 m³ per day [11].

Bioenergy processes also have some environmental im- pacts. Therefore, it is necessary to understand the benefits and disadvantages of biogas plants [12]. These environmental effects can be evaluated and analyzed by various tools and techniques. The Life Cycle Assessment (LCA) is the most modern and developed method. LCA is a crucial tool for monitoring and analyzing the environmental impact potential associated with a product or service in the entire of its life cycle. The life cycle method helps us find ways to generate the

energy we need without lessening the energy source and releasing greenhouse gases that contribute to climate change. LCA has been developed to comprehensively evaluate the environmental impact of AD or the various organic waste treatment, including animal manure and wastewater [13]. For example, several studies assessed the life cycle and environ- mental impacts of sustainability of slaughter beef processing [14,15] and the energy recovery of slaughterhouse AD treat- ment in the United States [16]. However, the LCA of the two- stage anaerobic biogas production system for commercial- scale and pilot-scale in Taiwan and Indonesia has not yet been investigated.

This study uses a life cycle assessment to assess and compare the energy production and environmental impacts of C-BP AD in Taiwan and S-BP AD in Indonesia. Therefore, the main objectives of this study were to (1) quantify the practical impacts and pollutant emissions of biogas production sys- items; (2) identify the potential of C-BP and S-BP production processes to develop ecological agriculture; and (3) compare the C-BP and S-BP systems designed and built by Feng Chia University with the S-BP implemented in Indonesia in Indonesia and the scale-up of C-BP which has installed in one farming at central Taiwan.

6: <header>Methodology </header>
The systems under assessment

This study was carried out in two locations. The C-BP commercial-scale biogas plant was installed at a moderate pig farm with 1500-2000 pigs yearly in Nantou County, Central Taiwan. The climate in Central Taiwan is mild, generally warm, and temperature between 10°-37 °C. Every pig can produce about 0.1 day of biogas. This anaerobic two-stage biogas production technology (HyMeTek) can increase the amount of biogas by thirty to forty percent and has high- efficiency power generation, which is very suitable for local small-scale pig farms. The average biogas production of this commercial plant is 300 m3/day. The solid-liquid separation in the traditional three-stage wastewater treatment system is performed after collecting pig manure and urine to separate solid waste from liquid manure. The solid is used for com- posting and fertilizing the plantation field; meanwhile, the liquid is distributed to an anaerobic digester to produce biogas.

The S-BP system was installed at a slaughterhouse in Bai- lang village, Manado City, North Sulawesi Province, Indonesia. Manado City has a tropical climate. The annual temperature ranges from 21°-33 The pilot plant area in slaughterhouse Bailang village has 10,000 The solid waste production per cow is 10-30 kg [11]. Central Taiwan's Commercial Biogas Plant (C-BP) system is installed. A settling basin was modeled in the AD system's C-BP to separate the swine manure from unwanted particles such as sand, stones, and other particles, and a regulation basin was built for mixing manure, sludge, and wastewater. In each basin of settling and regulation ba- sins, some pumps have been placed to drain the swine manure to the following processing, whose capacities are 8.95 kWh and 17.9 kWh, respectively.

Furthermore, the feedstock is processed in the 1st mixing tank using an electric pump of 17.9 kWh. In the 2nd AD tank, gas and liquid separation are carried out, which requires 22.4 and 16.41 kWh of electricity for anaerobic processes and desulfurization, respectively. After the 2nd AD tank process, the liquid part will be pumped into the aeration pool for wastewater treatment for further COD removal, which needs 30.68 kWh electricity pumps. Finally, biogas production re- sults are converted into electricity and supplied to the grid. In contrast, the digestate production of 60 3/day was trans- ported to the farm owner for fertilizer, and the liquid was treated in wastewater treatment to meet the government requirement. The operational data of C-BP were collected from the plants through daily data recording.

Institute of Green Products, Feng Chia University, designed the Symnergy Biogas Pilot Plant (S-BP) AD system in Manado City, Indonesia. Meanwhile, Global Xin Energy Cooperation, Ltd, Taiwan, was the company that provided all the equip- ment. The pilot plant system was mesophilic from 30° to 38 °C. The slaughterhouse wastewater and solid and liquid cow manure feedstock capacity was 2 m³ per day in the acido- genesis (hydrogen production) and methanogenesis (methane production) stages. The external heating was not installed because the S-BP's ambient temperature is already high enough to support the growth of mesophilic bacteria. How- ever, the heating system will not significantly impact the biogas production rate in tropical regions under the lower temperature gradient. The average daily production of the biogas was 1.3 m³, and the digestate was 2 m³ per day. The result of biogas production from S-BP was used for electrical, cooking fuel, and biofertilizer [11]. The S-BP system is placed in the slaughterhouse, modeled to include mixing, anaerobic digesters, biogas, and a biofertilizer pool. The main composi- tion of feedstock is cow dung and agricultural waste. The feedstock is pumped into the mixing process by a 1.21 kWh electricity pump for h daily. The total electric energy needed for the whole AD process was 1.5 kWh. The result of biogas production was used for cooking gas in the surrounding rural community and electricity to power the slaughterhouse. S-BP data were collected from local government surveys and in- terviews with the person in charge of the slaughterhouse.
7: This study was carried out in two locations. The C-BP commercial-scale biogas plant was installed at a moderate pig farm with 1500-2000 pigs yearly in Nantou County, Central Taiwan. The climate in Central Taiwan is mild, generally warm, and temperature between 10°-37 °C. Every pig can produce about 0.1 day of biogas. This anaerobic two-stage biogas production technology (HyMeTek) can increase the amount of biogas by thirty to forty percent and has high- efficiency power generation, which is very suitable for local small-scale pig farms. The average biogas production of this commercial plant is 300 m3/day. The solid-liquid separation in the traditional three-stage wastewater treatment system is performed after collecting pig manure and urine to separate solid waste from liquid manure. The solid is used for com- posting and fertilizing the plantation field; meanwhile, the liquid is distributed to an anaerobic digester to produce biogas.

The S-BP system was installed at a slaughterhouse in Bai- lang village, Manado City, North Sulawesi Province, Indonesia. Manado City has a tropical climate. The annual temperature ranges from 21°-33 The pilot plant area in slaughterhouse Bailang village has 10,000 The solid waste production per cow is 10-30 kg [11]. Central Taiwan's Commercial Biogas Plant (C-BP) system is installed. A settling basin was modeled in the AD system's C-BP to separate the swine manure from unwanted particles such as sand, stones, and other particles, and a regulation basin was built for mixing manure, sludge, and wastewater. In each basin of settling and regulation ba- sins, some pumps have been placed to drain the swine manure to the following processing, whose capacities are 8.95 kWh and 17.9 kWh, respectively.

Furthermore, the feedstock is processed in the 1st mixing tank using an electric pump of 17.9 kWh. In the 2nd AD tank, gas and liquid separation are carried out, which requires 22.4 and 16.41 kWh of electricity for anaerobic processes and desulfurization, respectively. After the 2nd AD tank process, the liquid part will be pumped into the aeration pool for wastewater treatment for further COD removal, which needs 30.68 kWh electricity pumps. Finally, biogas production re- sults are converted into electricity and supplied to the grid. In contrast, the digestate production of 60 3/day was trans- ported to the farm owner for fertilizer, and the liquid was treated in wastewater treatment to meet the government requirement. The operational data of C-BP were collected from the plants through daily data recording.

10: The study's feedstock characteristics, shown in Table 1, were collected from the farm and tested in the laboratory. The total solids (TS) were 62.3 g/L, VS was 43.3 g/L, COD concentration was 58,6 g/L in the C-BP, and, in the S-BP, were 71.1, 0.47, and 109.2 g/L, respectively [17]. The C-BP system uses swine manure and sludge as the main components of the feedstock. Meanwhile, S-BP uses cow dung and agricultural waste. The S- BP and C-BP laboratory performance of total nutrients was tested in the Manado city government laboratory and Feng Chia University laboratory. However, since laboratory equip- ment was limited in the S-BP area, some data with similar conditions and characteristics were taken from the literature about optimizing methane production by co-digestion of cow manure and organic waste as anaerobic digestion feedstock [18]. Fertilizers provide elements needed by plants to grow well, such as Nitrogen (N), phosphorus (P), and potassium (K). Therefore, it can make crops grow faster and more prominent

14: LCA is a tool to assess and analyze the environmental impacts of the commercial and pilot scale of the anaerobic two-stage biogas plant. LCA is widely used for evaluating the environ- mental impacts of products, processes or services over entire life cycle. The environmental impacts were evaluated following IS standards [19] with SimaPro v9.0.0 software. The LCA method typically involves four stages: goal and scope definition, inventory analysis, impact assessment, and inter- pretation [19].

During the goal and scope definition stage, the purpose of the assessment, system boundaries and functional unit are defined [20]. In the inventory analysis stage, data is collected on the inputs and outputs associated with each life cycle stage. The impact assessment stage evaluated the potential environmental impacts associated with the inputs and out- puts identified in the inventory analysis. Finally, the impact assessment results are evaluated in the interpretation stage, and conclusions are drawn [21].

Several studies about LCA have been conducted to investigate manure biogas production worldwide. In their LCA study, Esteves et al. [22] concluded that manure biogas production can provide significant environmental benefits, such as reducing greenhouse gas emissions and improving soil fertility. Meanwhile, Singh et al. [23], found that the biogas plant has a positive environmental performance, with a net reduction in greenhouse gas emissions and a positive energy balance. They also identify several key factors that influence the environmental performance of the plant, including the efficiency of the biogas production process, the composting of the sewage sludge and the transportation distance of the feedstock and digestate. More studies were reviewed in this study (Table This short review compared the factors and parameters addressed and the system performance.

This study was carried out using the CML-IA 2000 baseline v3.04 methodology. The CML stands for "Centrum voor Milieukunde Leiden", a research institute of the Centre for Environmental Studies at Leiden University in the Netherlands that developed a widely used method for LCA, called the CML method. It entails a series of environmental impact categories that are measured in terms of environ- mental emissions. The assessed environmental impact cate- gories were: Global Warming Potential (GWP), Acidification Potential (AP), Eutrophication Potential (EP), Photochemical Ozone Creation Potential (POCP), and Human Toxicity Poten- tial (HTP).
Goal and scope definitions The first stage of a life cycle assessment is defining the goal and the scope. This study aimed to evaluate the potential

environmental impacts of the anaerobic two-stage biogas system: (i) pilot plant and (ii) commercial plant. The functional unit (FU) describes the primary function of treating the different methods as functionally equivalent compared to the biogas production systems [35]. The product system assessed was anaerobic digestion of animal manure treatment in two biogas pilot and commercial plants. The primary function of the biogas system is to produce energy from manure fermentation. Therefore, the functional unit for life cycle assessment purposes in this study is the treatment of 10,950 tons per year of swine manure in C-BP (equal to 21 times of S- BP per year) that flows into the digester. Two types of biogas production systems were evaluated and analyzed: i) pilot plant system (S-BP) using the slaughterhouse waste (SHW) from the mixture of cow manure and agricultural waste; ii) commercial plant system (C-BP) using the mixture of pig manure and sludge (PMS). The boundary system of both biogas plants was classified into three phases: collecting, processing, and production (Fig. 1).

This study assessed a gate-to-grave LCA. Taking the manure for biogas utilization, anaerobic digestion process, and biogas production has been considered. The transport associated with bio-fertilization was not included. The boundary system did not include the GHG emissions from infrastructure, transportation of the equipment used in the whole system, maintenance and plant manufacturing, and decommissioning of the anaerobic digestion plants. They were reported to be a negligible impact per unit of biogas that was created during the equipment's lifetime uses [13,33]. Therefore, it was not considered.

19: The inventory steps include collecting, managing, and analyzing data to reach the goal. First, inventory analysis was done by listing all the input materials and products/byprod- ucts, consisting of a stream of input data, process, and output. Next, a literature review, laboratory measurement, and interview were conducted to collect biogas content, produc- tion, materials input weights, and digestate data. Finally, data collection and qualification were based on the data collected from 2019 to 2020 and complemented with the ecoinvent database version 3.8 and the relevant literature. The study has conducted the variant analysis of the mass fermentation mixing technology with a standard hydraulic pump, a mass and energy balance that determines each substrate's mass and energy distribution, and the biogas production at indi- vidual stages. Fig. 2 shows the process flow diagram mass and energy balance of C-BP and S-BP, and the operational data is provided in Table 2. The conversion and distribution process to end-users was not included in this study.

the direct emissions caused by diesel combustion exhaust in the transport phase. The data inventory of this stage is derived from the data provided by the Ecoinvent 3.8 database. The biogas production system's consumption of energy is based on the system's function. Therefore, it was calculated according to the input and output energy balance, considering the cleaning up and mixture of the feedstock and the electricity consumption for pumps and mixers. The C-BP uses a CSTR, which utilizes a mechanical transmission to maximize the entire system's function. On the other hand, S-BP is a small biogas pilot plant that uses manual work in some processes. However, the calculation of energy consumption on S-BP prioritizes only the use of electricity for pumps, as well as fuel for the transportation of cattle to the slaughterhouse. There- fore, the energy flows were calculated as natural and trans- formed resources. The feedstock that is input to the biogas digester in C-BP is swine manure, and in S-BP is cow dung. Both are obtained in the liquid form. The energy consumption of the biogas pro- duction system follows the function of the system. The biogas calorific value is 36 MJ/m³ on the methane content based. It means that one cubic meter of biogas is equivalent to 28.8 MJ

and 23.3 MJ of primary energy of C-BP and S-BP, respectively. The biogas yield is assumed to be stable, containing 72% CH4, 24% CO2, 1% N2 for C-BP, 65% CH4, 33%CO2, and 4.92% N2 for S- BP. Biogas leakage in the processing phase was 0.6% and 9.2% from C-BP and S-BP, respectively. Due to the limitation of laboratory tests, the N2 value was referred from Refs. [17,38], which has similar parameters to this study. According to the previous study of assessment of the slaughterhouse waste- water in Manado, Indonesia [11], the biowaste production of the slaughterhouse was 10-30 kg per day, and wastewater used as a substrate for biogas production in the Symnergy biogas pilot plant was 2 m³ per day.

in DCB-eq, freshwater aquatic ecotoxicity potential (FAETP) in DCB-eq, ozone layer depletion potential (ODP) described as trichlorofluoromethane equivalent (CFC-11-eq), marine aquatic ecotoxicity potential (MAETP) in DCB-eq photochem- ical ozone creation potential (POCP) as ethylene equivalent (C2H4), terrestrial ecotoxicity potential (TETP) expressed as 1,4-dichlorobenzene equivalent (DCB-eq), acidification po- tential (AP) described as sulfur dioxide equivalents (SO2-eq), and eutrophication potential (EP) described as phosphate equivalent (PO4-eq). GWP accounts for CO2, N2O, and CH4 emissions, measured in kg of CO2 equivalence (kg CO2-eq). AP

deals with the loss of calcium, magnesium, and potassium essential elements through oozing and changing hydrogen and aluminum (acidified elements). The potential acidifica- tion impacts came from the pollutants derived from Nitrogen and sulfur, such as NOx and NH3. It is measured in kg of SO2 equivalence (kg SO2-eq) [9]. Furthermore, EP is a process of surplus nutrients accumulations in the pond, river, or streaming water, which slowly move to enhance the plant's growth. These nutrients meet in the water bodies through biofertilizers, and it is expressed in kg of phosphate equivalent (kg PO4-eq) [39,40].

2: The LCA of two biogas systems, commercial and pilot plants, utilizing pig slurry and cow dung, were conducted. The emission was allocated and analyzed. In addition, the energy balance and impacts on the environment of the S-BP and C-BP were analyzed. Emissions from collecting and mixing manure and digestate include N2O, NH3, and CH4. There are about 5.99% of Nitrogen, and 5.49% of carbon were initially lost during the manure-collecting process in the C-BP. The CH4 and CO2 manure emissions were 14.29% and 85.71% during the collecting phase [41]. However, the digestate emissions for the production phase were 34.5% and 57.3%. NOx, N2O, and NH3 emissions were 0.76%, 1.1%, and 98.14% of the nitrogen loss, respectively [42,43]. The initial total carbon and Nitrogen lost in the S-BP were 6.87% and 7.93% [44]. The CO2 and CH4 digestate emissions for the production phase were 36.6% and 61.4% [29,45]. NOx, N2O, and NH3 emissions were 0.93%, 2.02%, and 77.1% of the nitrogen loss, respectively [46]. Table 3
summarizes the pollutant emissions inventory for the C-BP and S-BP biogas plants.

The manure retention from the farming installations and the subsequent waste storage cause significant emissions. The pollutant emissions in each process can be divided into direct and indirect emissions. Direct emission occurs during the process of biogas production, and it also occurs in the collecting feedstock and process phases. Meanwhile, indirect emissions result from activities that occur during energy us- ages, such as the emission from biogas burning, emission from fuel, and electricity consumption. During storage, solid fertilizers or liquid slurries tend to release some of the gas into the atmosphere caused by the activity of the bacteria. The main gas released during the decomposition of the manure was methane, about 5084.63 kg CO2-eq. Ammonia emissions are associated with two major environmental problems: Acidification and Eutrophication. In addition, the nitrogen compounds such as N2O, NH3, and NO in the C-BP collecting process are 0.201 kg CO2-eq, 430.75 kg CO2-eq, and 33.37 kg CO2-eq, respectively. Hence, for the S-BP collection phase, a CO pollutant emission of 6.5 kg CO2-eq came from feedstock transportation to the location.
Table 3 - Pollutant emission inventory of S-BP and C-BP (in kg/functional unit).

0: The methane production as biogas during the manure treatment process in the anaerobic digester was collected and distributed to the gas holder for the desulfurization process for power and heat production. In this case, biogas is released accidently through a 0.6% and 9.2% leak in the C-BP and S-BP digesters, respectively. As a result, digestate emissions of 130.9 kg CO2-eq CH4, 61.36 kg CO2-eq, and 2.59 kg CO2-eq NH3 in C-BP, as well as the amount of 102.53 kg CO2-eq CH4, 49.61 kg CO2-eq, and 4.29 kg CO2-eq nitrogen oxides in S-BP, occurred during the biogas processing and fertilizer produc- tion processes. Considering carbon dioxide, the combustion of biogas leads to efficient methane oxidation and conversion to CO2, with a calorific value of 26.8 MJ/m³ in C-BP and 23.3 MJ/m³ in S-BP (Table 2) according to the methane content of biogas. Methane emissions come from livestock and other agricul- tural practices, land use, and organic waste in municipal solid waste landfills or feedstock. Electricity consumption is mainly for the pumps in mixing and AD processes.
The LCA analysis showed that it depends on the type of impact categories. The different unit processes have another dominant environmental impact potential. The environmental

impact categories of the two systems on the GWP, Human Toxicity, Photochemical Oxidation, Acidification, and Eutro- phication, were observed for the biogas production processes, from the collecting and mixing feedstock, anaerobic digestion of biogas production and digestion processes (Fig. 3). According to the analysis, the collecting feedstock impacts GWP 54% and Eutrophication 55%, either in C-BP. Human Toxicity has the highest environmental impact for the biogas production pro- cess at 60.89%. The environmental impact of Human Toxicity can be affected by several factors, including the size of the plant, the feedstock treatment and handling, and the emissions control technologies employed. Without adequate handling and control, the release of toxic gases can have a significant impact on human toxicity.

2554.3 tons of Nitrogen in the 10,950 ton slurry feedstock of C- BP and 121.77 tons of Nitrogen in 522 ton slurry feedstock of S- BP per year. However, the assumption of the digestate content is to be about 3.38 g N/kg slurry feedstock fed to the digester. According to the calculation based on the operational data, nitrogen content in the C-BP digestate was 2398.27 tons of Nitrogen, and 114.32 tons of Nitrogen in the S-BP slurry feed- stock fed into the digester. The score for collecting feedstock, biogas production, and the total score of CML-IA baseline midpoint impact categories was shown in Table 4 for C-BP and Table 5 for S-BP.

Regarding the carbon emissions, it was found that the total amount of GWP in producing biogas production of 109,500 m³ per year at C-BP was 1.982 kt CO2-eq. Meanwhile, the GWP amount for producing 339.3 m³ per year of biogas production at S-BP was 5.67 tons CO2-eq. The biggest contributor to GWP was CO2 emissions. The result shows that the C-BP system had a GWP emission of 0.0181 kg CO2-eq for producing 1 m³ of biogas from swine manure and sludge. Meanwhile, S-BP had a GWP emission of 0.0167 kg CO2-eq for producing 1 m³ biogas from cow manure and slaughterhouse waste. This result is close to the study of Zhang et al. [48], a typical household biogas system with a digester volume of 8 m³ in China rural

area, which found that the total CO2 emission was 0.02kgCO2- eq. Previous studies have been conducted on various types of digesters and parameters. It was reported by Garfi et al. that the GWP impact for the small-scale tubular plastic digesters in the Caribbean was 4.83-5.93 kg CO2-eq [49], and Wang et al. found that the 8 m³ household biogas digesters had the GWP impact of 366.87 kg CO2-eq [50]. Gabisa and Gheewala [51] reported that the fixed-dome biogas digester in Ethiopia emits 0.33 tons CO2-eq every year. A similar study was reported by Ioannou et al. [52] and Vu et al. [53].

7: <header>Acidification, eutrophication, human toxicity, and Photochemical Oxidation </header>

Regarding Acidification potential, Eutrophication, and Photo- chemical potentials, the impact system was mainly due to air emissions from fertilizer and digestate application, digestate production, and manure storage. Acidification from the dispersion of SOx, NOx, HF, and HCl compounds in the waste can cause acid rain. In addition, biogas, as an alternative en- ergy source, directly reduced CO2 emissions and, as an alter- native fertilizer of the digestate, indirectly. Tables 4 and 5 show the C-BP and S-BP impact potential determined during
Fig. 4 - Total environmental impact percentage between C-BP and S-BP.
biogas production. In Fig. 4 showed that C-BP had potential impacts on Photochemical Ozone Creation Potential 33%, GWP 27%, and Eutrophication 23%.

The POCP is a measure of a substance's potential to contribute to the formation of the ground-level ozone when it reacts with sunlight. Biogas production contributes highly to POCP by emitting volatile organic compounds (VOC) from various sources such as digester leakage or the organic waste feedstock during biogas production and storage. The reaction of VOC and sunlight have form the ground-level ozone that affect Human Toxicity (HTP) and the environment. There are several ways to reduce the high POCP impact, such as regu- larly inspecting the biogas system and repairing any leaks to reduce the VOC emissions and prevent the reaction with sunlight; Implementing odor control measures with biofilters, or installing the gas cleaning filters or scrubbers to help to remove VOC before it released to the environment.

The Eutrophication was mainly due to field emissions, such as nitrate leaching, and ammonia and phosphate emis- sions [54] caused by wastewater discharge. The standard quality of wastewater treatment was developed for total Ni- trogen and phosphorus content, whereas the eutrophication potential of treated wastewater is determined by non-organic nutrient forms contained in the discharged wastewater that is used for vegetation. Biogas production from organic waste has released NH3 and NO gases contributing to AP and EP. When these gases are released into the air or enter the water bodies, it causes an imbalance in the pH and nutrient levels, respec- tively. To mitigate the high impact of EP, recovering nutrients such as N, P, and K from the organic waste in biogas produc- tion is helpful to prevent their release into the environment. Referring to section 2.1.1, the value of Nitrogen from S-BP is 0.39%, which is three times higher than the Nitrogen in the C- BP, which was 0.13%. Also, the Pottasium in S-BP was 14.5% which is five times higher than C-BP of 3.45%. These com- pounds are essential for plants, and it can mitigate the high contribution to EP in the S-BP.

The highest environmental impact category in the S-BP during biogas production was Human Toxicity 58% and Acidification 48%. The conditions at S-BP, without purification systems installed, and the collection process of raw material feedstock (manure) still in open storage, significantly impact the Human Toxicity category. In addition, the treatment of effluent waste from biogas requires an adequate wastewater treatment system. The unavailability of the wastewater treatment system results in excess effluent being accommo- dated in the biofertilizer pond, some of which flows directly back into the river.

Meanwhile, biofertilizers as byproducts still need to be handled better to reduce the unfavorable impact on plants. Regarding biofertilizers, it is necessary to conduct laboratory analysis for the results of biofertilizer content as byproducts. Biofertilizers can be related to Eutrophication if they contain excess nutrients such as Nitrogen and phosphorus. When these nutrients are released into water bodies, they can harm the aquatic ecosystem and the plantation. Furthermore, a higher potential for Eutrophication, Acidification, and human toxicity has resulted from the more elevated ammonia and sulfur dioxide emissions that formed in the pilot-scale plant. According to the applied methodological approach of the

biogas production process, the environmental impact is mostly related to CO2 emission and other chemical elements in the atmosphere during biogas combustion. Additionally, if the feedstock used in the biogas production process is contaminated with poisonous substances, it is also one factor that increases the impact on human toxicity. However, it can be mitigated through the emissions control technologies such as scrubbers and filters and the careful se- lection and handling of the feedstock. The high impacts on Acidification and Eutrophication were due to the potential release of nitrogen and sulfur compounds during anaerobic digestion. These compounds can be converted into nitric and sulfuric acid, contributing to Acidification when released into the environment. Additionally, using certain types of feed- stocks in biogas production, such as manure, can release excess nutrients such as Nitrogen and phosphorus, contrib- uting to Eutrophication when released into the water bodies. Photochemical Ozone Creation Potential has a high impact during the collecting feedstock and biogas production. The impact was generated by unintentional leaks in the digester and during the discharge processes of the digestate.

There are several ways to reduce the high POCP impact, such as regu- larly inspecting the biogas system and repairing any leaks to reduce the VOC emissions and prevent the reaction with sunlight; Implementing odor control measures with biofilters, or installing the gas cleaning filters or scrubbers to help to remove VOC before it released to the environment.

The Eutrophication was mainly due to field emissions, such as nitrate leaching, and ammonia and phosphate emis- sions [54] caused by wastewater discharge. The standard quality of wastewater treatment was developed for total Ni- trogen and phosphorus content, whereas the eutrophication potential of treated wastewater is determined by non-organic nutrient forms contained in the discharged wastewater that is used for vegetation. Biogas production from organic waste has released NH3 and NO gases contributing to AP and EP. When these gases are released into the air or enter the water bodies, it causes an imbalance in the pH and nutrient levels, respec- tively. To mitigate the high impact of EP, recovering nutrients such as N, P, and K from the organic waste in biogas produc- tion is helpful to prevent their release into the environment. Referring to section 2.1.1, the value of Nitrogen from S-BP is 0.39%, which is three times higher than the Nitrogen in the C- BP, which was 0.13%. Also, the Pottasium in S-BP was 14.5% which is five times higher than C-BP of 3.45%. These com- pounds are essential for plants, and it can mitigate the high contribution to EP in the S-BP.

The highest environmental impact category in the S-BP during biogas production was Human Toxicity 58% and Acidification 48%. The conditions at S-BP, without purification systems installed, and the collection process of raw material feedstock (manure) still in open storage, significantly impact the Human Toxicity category. In addition, the treatment of effluent waste from biogas requires an adequate wastewater treatment system. The unavailability of the wastewater treatment system results in excess effluent being accommo- dated in the biofertilizer pond, some of which flows directly back into the river.

Meanwhile, biofertilizers as byproducts still need to be handled better to reduce the unfavorable impact on plants. Regarding biofertilizers, it is necessary to conduct laboratory analysis for the results of biofertilizer content as byproducts. Biofertilizers can be related to Eutrophication if they contain excess nutrients such as Nitrogen and phosphorus. When these nutrients are released into water bodies, they can harm the aquatic ecosystem and the plantation. Furthermore, a higher potential for Eutrophication, Acidification, and human toxicity has resulted from the more elevated ammonia and sulfur dioxide emissions that formed in the pilot-scale plant. According to the applied methodological approach of the

biogas production process, the environmental impact is mostly related to CO2 emission and other chemical elements in the atmosphere during biogas combustion. Additionally, if the feedstock used in the biogas production process is contaminated with poisonous substances, it is also one factor that increases the impact on human toxicity. However, it can be mitigated through the emissions control technologies such as scrubbers and filters and the careful se- lection and handling of the feedstock. The high impacts on Acidification and Eutrophication were due to the potential release of nitrogen and sulfur compounds during anaerobic digestion. These compounds can be converted into nitric and sulfuric acid, contributing to Acidification when released into the environment. Additionally, using certain types of feed- stocks in biogas production, such as manure, can release excess nutrients such as Nitrogen and phosphorus, contrib- uting to Eutrophication when released into the water bodies. Photochemical Ozone Creation Potential has a high impact during the collecting feedstock and biogas production. The impact was generated by unintentional leaks in the digester and during the discharge processes of the digestate.

Acidification from the dispersion of SOx, NOx, HF, and HCl compounds in the waste can cause acid rain. In addition, biogas, as an alternative en- ergy source, directly reduced CO2 emissions and, as an alter- native fertilizer of the digestate, indirectly. 12: Following the analysis, the total environmental impact per- centage between C-BP and S-BP can be found in Fig. 4. The highest impact category for the C-BP system in all stages of the biogas production process is Photochemical Ozone Creation Potential at 33%. It is various conditions cause the impact on Photochemical Oxidation. The release of volatile organic compounds and nitrogen oxides during the handling and treatment of the feedstock and digestate is also one of the reasons for the high impact on Photochemical Oxidation. In addition, feedstock stored in open storage can cause Photo- chemical Ozone Creation Potential effects. If the feedstock is open and exposed to direct sunlight, the organic matter in the feedstock will undergo a photochemical reaction with oxygen in the air.

The highest impact category for the S-BP system in all stages of the biogas production process is Eutrophication 33%. Eutrophication occurs when excess nutrients are introduced to water bodies. The sewage treatment system from the S-BP system in the slaughterhouse has not been appropriately handled. After the anaerobic digestion, the remaining waste- water goes directly to the holding pond to be used as a bio- fertilizer. The wastewater must be analyzed further to deter- mine the nutrient content that meets the required standards. However, the existing condition is that if the wastewater pond is full, the water will flow directly into the river and agricul- tural land around the system, which causes a high Eutrophi- cation impact on the S-BP system. But, overall, both systems of C-BP and S-BP have meager impacts, either on climate change or water depletion.

In addition, feedstock stored in open storage can cause Photo- chemical Ozone Creation Potential effects. The highest impact category for the S-BP system in all stages of the biogas production process is Eutrophication 33%. Eutrophication occurs when excess nutrients are introduced to water bodies. After the anaerobic digestion, the remaining waste- water goes directly to the holding pond to be used as a bio- fertilizer. The wastewater must be analyzed further to deter- mine the nutrient content that meets the required standards. However, the existing condition is that if the wastewater pond is full, the water will flow directly into the river and agricul- tural land around the system, which causes a high Eutrophi- cation impact on the S-BP system. 14: This study scope does not include fertilizer transport, prod- ucts, and land applications. Moreover, could substantially impact environmental emissions. Tthis study did not include

0: any type of supply chain, manufacturing, and biogas system installation that could substantially impact environmental emissions. The life cycle assessment of anaerobic two-stage biogas production processes between commercial and pilot plants has been evaluated and analyzed. As a result, Photo- chemical Ozone Creation Potential had the highest total impact on C-BP (33%), and Eutrophication had the highest impact on S-BP (33%). This is because C-BP has a longer operational time with a higher production rate than S-BP in a certain period. Therefore, if the digester is used longer, the CO2 emission reduction benefits will be even more significant. However, the biogas production system of C-BP and S-BP showed very low environmental impacts, and the impacts of C-BP are lower than the S-BP. To mitigate the high contribution to POCP, there are several ways to implement such as regularly inspecting the biogas system and repairing any leaks to reduce the VOC emissions and prevent the reaction with sunlight; Implementing odor control measures with biofilters, or installing gas cleaning filters or scrubbers to help to remove VOC before it released to the environment. Meanwhile, to reduce the high impact of EP, it is necessary to recover the nutrients content such as N, P, and K from the organic waste in biogas production to prevent it from the release into the environment. However, this study found that the two-stage systems of C-BP and S-BP have negligible impacts on climate change or water depletion. In November 2021, the Global Methane Pledge was launched at COP 26 to catalyze the action to reduce methane emissions. Therefore, the two-stage biogas system is a good idea for the policy maker to treat the methane production problem. In addition, the policymaker may set up some incentives through this kind of research to encourage the farmer asso- ciations to set up the high-efficiency anaerobic digestion system to treat the agricultural waste and manures from the farm to reduce CO2 emissions. This study evaluates and an- alyzes the commercial and pilot biogas plant environmental sustainability in Taiwan and Indonesia. Based on the study, it is necessary to suggest recommendations to the decision- makers from an environmental sustainability point of view for GHG emission reduction. At the same time, there are more opportunities for future research that focus on the bioenergy sector's social acceptance and policy awareness, primarily for advanced biogas system development.
